name: United Coffee House
why_it_matters: Connaught Place's oldest surviving restaurant — high ceilings, leather banquettes, and the same tomato fish recipe from 1962. Where Delhi conducted business lunches before there was business.
insider_tip: Order the cona coffee — brewed glass-on-glass in a vacuum siphon, table-side. They've made it the same way since opening.
signature_address: E-15, Connaught Place, Outer Circle, New Delhi 110001

name: Wenger's
why_it_matters: Designed by Robert Tor Russell, the architect of Connaught Place itself — the first Delhi bakery to bring Swiss chocolates and continental pastry. Now Tandon-family run; the queue at the counter is part of the experience.
insider_tip: It's takeaway-only — there's no seating. Order the chicken patty fresh from the morning trays; afternoon batches lose the flake.
signature_address: A-16, Inner Circle, Connaught Place, New Delhi 110001

name: Embassy Restaurant
why_it_matters: Set up in 1948 by two Karachi-restaurant exiles a year after Partition. The tandoori chicken recipe came with them and hasn't changed. Often credited with introducing chana bhatura to Delhi.
insider_tip: The dal meat is the dish locals order; the menu emphasises tandoori but the slow-cooked mutton dal is the kitchen's quiet legend.
signature_address: 11-D, Connaught Place, Inner Circle, New Delhi 110001

name: Saravana Bhavan
why_it_matters: Chennai's Saravanaa Bhavan brought to CP — pure-veg, hyper-consistent, the dosas land in three minutes flat. The Janpath outlet is where North Indians come to learn what real sambar tastes like.
insider_tip: Leave your name at the entrance and wait — the system feels chaotic but moves fast. Filter coffee at the end is non-negotiable.
signature_address: 46, Janpath Road, Connaught Place, New Delhi 110001

name: Kake Da Hotel
why_it_matters: A 1931 dhaba that refused to dhaba-up — same charcoal-cooked butter chicken, same crowded bench seating, same families running it. The CP institution that locals correct tourists about.
insider_tip: Their butter chicken is gravy-thin compared to the global cream-bomb version; ask for an extra tandoori roti to mop the corners of the karahi.
signature_address: 67, Outer Circle, Municipal Market, Connaught Place, New Delhi 110001

name: Indian Coffee House
why_it_matters: Delhi outpost of India's worker-owned coffee co-operative since 1957. Closed during the Emergency (1975-77), reopened at Mohan Singh Place where it's been ever since. White-uniformed waiters with starched turbans, prices frozen in another decade.
insider_tip: Take the lift to the second floor and grab a window table — the rooftop view of CP's inner circle is the unannounced reason to come.
signature_address: 2nd Floor, Mohan Singh Place, Baba Kharak Singh Marg, Connaught Place, New Delhi 110001

name: Andhra Bhavan Canteen
why_it_matters: The state government's canteen for visiting officers — opened to the public, became a Delhi institution. Unlimited Andhra thali for ₹200 with kandi podi, gongura pickle, and rasam — nothing in the city compares for value.
insider_tip: Lunch goes from 12-3pm; arrive by 12:30 or you'll wait 45 minutes in the corridor. Non-veg is in a separate room — order chicken or mutton fry à la carte alongside the veg thali.
signature_address: 1, Ashok Road, near Feroze Shah Road, New Delhi 110001

name: Berco's
why_it_matters: Where Delhi went for its first proper Indo-Chinese in the 80s. Now 50+ branches but the CP original still defines the comfort dish — chilli chicken slick enough to coat hakka noodles.
insider_tip: The dimsum platter is hit-or-miss; stick to the wok dishes which is what made the brand. Lunch buffet runs noon-3pm if you want quantity.
signature_address: E-42/43, Inner Circle, Rajiv Chowk, Connaught Place, New Delhi 110001

name: Kwality Restaurant
why_it_matters: Started as Pishori Lal Lamba's ice-cream counter in 1940; American GIs in WWII shaped its dessert side (cassata, tutti frutti). Reopened in 2019 with green awnings and red velvet curtains, channelling its Raj-era looks.
insider_tip: Yes the chole bhature is the Delhi staple, but the original draw was the ice-cream — finish with a sicilian cassata and you'll understand why CP families came here for Sunday outings since 1940.
signature_address: 7, Regal Building, Parliament Street, Connaught Place, New Delhi 110001

name: Bengali Sweet House
why_it_matters: One of the first shops in Delhi to bring rasgulla north from Bengal — the namesake of the entire Bengali Market. Crisp pani puris, cham cham, kachori; a clean canteen-style chaat experience that families in central Delhi grew up on.
insider_tip: There's also Bhimsain's Bengali Sweet House nearby with the same name — locals call this one 'the original' to distinguish. Stand in the chaat queue, not the sweet counter, if you only have ten minutes.
signature_address: 27/30, Bengali Market, Tansen Marg, Mandi House, New Delhi 110001
